evidence: WordSketchApiServer uses Builder pattern with explicit dependency injection; all collaborators (QueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader) arrive through the builder, not via globals.
Main.handleServerCommand() builds the object graph in a single explicit sequence: executor -> grammarConfig -> server; no hidden boot-order magic.

Static members across the codebase are pure constants (magic numbers, logger, regex Pattern literals) or immutable maps initialized in static blocks with no I/O — SimpleTagger's static VERB_FORMS block is data-only.

SemanticFieldExplorer provides a two-constructor design: one that opens a BlackLabIndex itself (SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath)), and one that accepts an injected executor for testing — but the API server always calls the self-opening constructor.

In WordSketchApiServer, three request handlers (/api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/compare, and the explore-multi path) each call 'new SemanticFieldExplorer(indexPath)' directly, which opens a new BlackLab index connection mid-request instead of reusing the shared this.executor.

None of the three SemanticFieldExplorer usages in WordSketchApiServer are wrapped in try-with-resources; close() is called on the happy path only, so any exception between construction and close() leaks the index handle.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() hardcodes a relative path 'grammars/relations.json' — callers must run from the project root; this factory is never called in the main codebase but is a latent CWD-dependent init trap.

grammarConfig is initialized to null in Main.handleServerCommand() (graceful optional load); this propagates through 8+ null-guards in WordSketchApiServer request handlers rather than being resolved to a null-object or explicit Optional at startup.

strengths: Explicit builder-pattern construction in WordSketchApiServer keeps all init dependencies visible and avoids global singletons.
Main.java builds the object graph in a clear top-down sequence with no hidden cross-class boot-order dependencies.
Static initializers (SimpleTagger.VERB_FORMS, constant Pattern literals) are pure data — no I/O, network calls, or environment variable reads at class-load time.
BlackLabQueryExecutor opens its index in the constructor and is shutdown-hook-closed in Main, which is a straightforward lifecycle with no deferred or lazy coupling.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: A single repeated pattern dominates: the API server bypasses its own injected executor and re-opens the BlackLab index from scratch for every semantic-field request, inverting the clean construction discipline the rest of the codebase maintains.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the worst initialization_coupling traps — no Java static initializers with I/O, no global singletons with creation-order problems, no circular init dependencies, and no environment reads at class-load time. What pulls the score down is a consistent but localized breach: three request handlers each construct a new SemanticFieldExplorer that opens a fresh BlackLab index handle, bypassing the shared executor that is already wired through the builder, and none of them use try-with-resources, so any exception on the exploration path leaks the handle. This is not a systemic architectural failure but it is a clear, repeated violation of the initialization discipline otherwise present — analogous to a 'solid but uneven' 80-level codebase with a notable recurring mistake in one subsystem, landing closer to 77.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer opened per-request in API server, bypassing the shared executor and leaking on exceptions
evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java line ~530: 'SemanticFieldExplorer explorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(indexPath);' — this calls SemanticFieldExplorer(String), which calls 'new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath)' which calls 'BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath))', opening the index from disk on every request.
The same pattern appears at lines ~786 and ~864 for compare and explore-multi paths.
In all three sites close() is called only on the happy path — if 'explorer.exploreByPattern(...)' throws, the index handle is never closed.
WordSketchApiServer already holds 'this.executor' (a shared BlackLabQueryExecutor) which could be passed to the testable 'SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor)' constructor instead.
issues.suggestion: Pass this.executor to SemanticFieldExplorer at all three call sites: 'try (SemanticFieldExplorer explorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(this.executor))'. This reuses the already-open shared index, removes the per-request I/O, and the try-with-resources guarantees close() even on exception paths.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() silently depends on CWD being the project root
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.java line ~169: 'return new GrammarConfigLoader(Path.of("grammars/relations.json"));' — relative path resolved against process CWD.
Any caller (test runner, tool, future service) that doesn't happen to run from the project root will receive an IllegalStateException with no guidance on what CWD was expected.
The factory is unused in the current codebase, making it a latent trap for the next developer who reaches for a 'convenient default'.
issues.suggestion: Remove createDefaultEnglish() or replace it with a version that resolves the path relative to a caller-supplied base directory or a classpath resource, and document the assumption explicitly: 'public static GrammarConfigLoader createDefault(Path projectRoot)'.

root_causes: The testable two-constructor design in SemanticFieldExplorer (injected executor vs self-opening) was not extended to the production call sites — the API server consistently uses the self-opening variant despite holding a suitable shared executor.
Nullable grammarConfig is managed by defensive null-checks scattered across request handlers rather than being resolved at startup into an explicit Optional or null-object pattern.
